questions.question: Define a process and explain its components.
keyPoints: Process is a program in execution
Components include: program code, data, stack, heap, and PCB
Represents an active entity with associated resources

questions.question: What is Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four information stored in PCB.
keyPoints: PCB is a kernel data structure storing process information
Stores: Process ID, Process State, CPU Registers, Memory Management Info
Also stores: I/O Status, Scheduling Information, Accounting Data

questions.question: Explain the five states of a process with a state transition diagram.
keyPoints: NEW: Process being created
READY: Waiting in queue for CPU
RUNNING: Currently executing
BLOCKED/WAITING: Waiting for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Execution completed
Transitions occur based on scheduler decisions and I/O operations

questions.question: Differentiate between a process and a program.
keyPoints: Program is passive (static file), Process is active (in execution)
Process has execution context, program does not
Multiple processes can run same program simultaneously
Process requires OS resources, program is just code

questions.question: What is context switching? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Context switch is saving state of one process and loading another
Involves saving CPU registers, program counter to PCB
Loading new process's PCB to CPU
Example: Time slice expiry causes scheduler to switch to another process

questions.question: Explain the difference between zombie and orphan processes.
keyPoints: Zombie: Process terminated but parent hasn't read exit status
Orphan: Process whose parent terminated before child
Zombies remain in process table consuming PID
Orphans are adopted by init process in Unix

questions.question: What is a daemon process? Give two examples.
keyPoints: Daemon runs in background without terminal control
Started at system boot, runs until shutdown
Examples: httpd (web server), cron (scheduled tasks), cupsd (print daemon), sshd (secure shell)

questions.question: Explain the process creation mechanism in operating systems. Discuss the steps involved and the system calls used in Unix/Linux for creating a new process.
keyPoints: Process creation involves: assigning PID, allocating memory, creating PCB, loading program
In Unix: fork() creates new process by duplicating parent
After fork(), exec() replaces child process image with new program
Parent uses wait() to synchronize with child termination
fork() returns twice: once in parent (with child PID) and once in child (with 0)

questions.question: Explain the role of process scheduler and dispatcher in process management. Discuss short-term, medium-term, and long-term schedulers.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: Admits processes from job pool to ready queue (controls degree of multiprogramming)
Short-term scheduler/CPU scheduler: Selects process from ready queue for execution (fast)
Medium-term scheduler: Swaps processes in/out of memory for load balancing
Dispatcher: Performs actual context switch, transfers CPU to selected process
Dispatcher latency: Time to stop one process and start another

questions.question: Draw and explain the process state transition diagram showing all possible transitions between New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Terminated states.
keyPoints: Diagram shows 5 states with transitions between them
New → Ready: OS admits process
Ready → Running: Scheduler dispatches process
Running → Ready: Time slice expired or preempted
Running → Blocked: Process initiates I/O or waits for event
Blocked → Ready: I/O completed or event occurred
Running → Terminated: Process completes or exits
Include explanation of each transition and conditions triggering them

questions.question: What is Process Control Block? Explain its structure and the information it contains. Why is PCB essential for process management?
keyPoints: PCB is kernel data structure representing a process
Contains: Process ID, State, Program Counter, CPU Registers, Memory Info
Also: I/O Status, Open Files, Scheduling Info, Accounting Data, Pointers
Essential because: OS needs to track process state, resume execution, manage resources
PCB enables context switching and process scheduling
Each process has unique PCB identified by PID
